title_of_original: Case C-382/99 - Kingdom of the Netherlands v Commission of the European Communities
decided_by: Court of Justice of the European Union
case_description: [1] On 15 March 2021, the Polish Government decided to increase excise duties on petrol starting from 1 April 2021 [2] and introduced a temporary subsidy for petrol sales at service stations near the Czech border, [3] aimed at maintaining price competitiveness. [4] Operators located within 20 km of the border received PLN 0.08 per litre [5] and those located 20–40 km from the border received PLN 0.04 per litre. [6] The subsidy was granted per service station, allowing operators with multiple stations to receive multiple payments. [6] The total amount was capped at EUR 100,000 over three years [7] and financed from the state budget. [8] Many service stations in the border region have exclusive purchasing agreements with oil companies. [9] These agreements include price management clauses [10] that require oil companies to bear the cost of forecourt discounts in response to market conditions, including competition with Czech fuel prices. [11] In practice, where subsidies were paid, oil companies covered only the remaining part of the discount beyond the amount covered by the aid.
